Name: Prasat Kuk Svay
Composition: Single shrine with a pond at north side
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite scatter
Description: Many brick and laterite blocks are scattered. The structure is completely disturbed by looting, and the original position of the brick structure could not be adequately determined. Many decorated stone objects are scattered around.

Name: Prasat Kuk Svay
Condition: Mound with brick and some stone elements
Description: Contemporary Nak Ta shrine stands on this mound. The building itself is destroyed and a small number of the brick pieces are scattered around. Local people collected soil from here and the southern part of this mound was destroyed recently. They said that some iron pieces were found in this soil.

Name: Wat Chas
Condition: Mound with brick, stone elements and looted hole
Description: There are some scattered bricks, 4 sandstone basements of boundary stone (Sima) and one panel of sandstone (B000U02) which represents the gods of 9 planets around this monument. Because of the existence of boundary stones, this monument is assumed to be constructed in the post Angkor period. This sandstone is reused because other faces are sculpted with floral decorations that are identified in Pre-Angkor style. Mysteriously in spite of this sandstone panel being found here in 2004 and moved into conservation storehouse in Sambor Prei Kuk, this element was recorded in an old photo taken in the 1960s in the storeroom.

Name: Prasat Lok Yeai
Condition: Mound with brick and looted hole
Description: This is an unexcavated mound with many scattered bricks. On the mound, one line of brick pavement was confirmed which suggested it to be part of a wall. There is another mound without brick at the north side of this mound.

Name: Tuol Ta Dong
Condition: Mound with brick
Description: This mound is located at the eastern side of Boeung Chea Kay pond. This is an unexcavated mound with a small number of scattered brick fragments. On the mound, some modern bricks (burned in French period) were confirmed. The surrounding of this mound is depressed. Local belief is that this mound persists with the spirit of Nak-Ta, and on which one wooden shrine has been erected.

Name: Prasat Kong Veang
Condition: Remain with the upper half of wall and platform, and looted hole
Description: This mound is on the opposite side of M.005, western side of Boeng Chea Kay pond. Many sandstone objects are scattered on the mound. The most important stone is a lintel in Sambor Prei Kuk style. Wooden shrine for the local belief for Nak-Ta is located near this building.

Name: Prasat Chreav
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: This mound is covered by accumulated brick and laterite. Most of the laterite block is fragmented and has lost original shape. The depth of looted hole is about 2.3m and a brick wall remains at the east and north side of the hole. There is one pond at the eastern side of this mound.

Name: Prasat Anlong Damrei
Composition: Single shrine with a pond at east side
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: This mound is covered by accumulated brick, laterite and pebbles. At the northern slope, many different size pebbles are confirmed. There are two looted holes on it. Dimension of the central hole is 2.5m (N-S) by 2m (E-W) and 2m depth. Inside the hole, the northern brick wall remained at 0.6m in height and the structures around the entrances are 0.7m in height. There is one pond at the eastern side of this mound.

Name: N31 Group
Composition: 7 shrines and 1 pond at northeast side
Description: This group of temple (M.9), M.11 and M.12 are located on the higher land. When worshippers access here from east side, they are gradually ascending the slope to the temples. Seven shrines align on a NNE-SSW line despite many temples face to east or slightly counterclockwise direction (15 degree from true east). One pond with laterite embankment is located at the northeast side of this temple group. Water channel which started from O Krou Ke river to Seng river pass at the north of this group to WSW direction. It is probably that the orientation of this group is planned to face parallel to this river. The seven structures are severely damaged, and no dating material such as inscription and decorative element have never found from this group.

Condition: Mound with brick
Mound with brick
Mound with brick, stone element and looted hole
Description: Interior brick wall is confirmed partially. Door frame remained and opens to the east direction.

Condition: Mound with brick and looted hole
Description: Interior brick wall is confirmed partially.

Condition: Mound with brick
Mound with brick and looted hole
Description: One looted hole remains at the center is 3.4m deep. Brick wall partially appears in this hole.

Condition: Mound with brick
Description: This is smallest mound in this group. Small amount of the brick is scattered on the mound.

Name: Prasat Sandan
Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: A pathway to M.009 and M.011 and M.012 is cut across in this temple enclosure wall. Orientation of this temple is rotated counterclockwise (approximately 8 degree from true east). Enclosure laterite wall (approx. 85m EW direction, 65m NS direction) is partially confirmed although nearly all part is collapsed. Several number of cylindrical shaped laterite blocks are scattered at the east side. Plan of this shrine is rectangular with double projection at each side. 3 tiers of roof remains. Some of the edicule decorations at the upper layer are still preserved. Decorative curving on the exterior wall, Lintel, Colonnette, Pilaster, and figures in each Flying Palace, might be chipped off in the later period. Decorative stucco is partially remained only on the flying palace, and curving on the brick and stucco decoration is different. Therefore, all removed decorations were changed by the new finishing material in the later period. Interior is completely damaged by previous looting. Some of the dangerous trees near the shrine were removed by SCP.

Name: N31 Group
Description: It is unclear if these shrines consisted of a single complex or individual shrines, or, these four shrines belong to M.12 temple. Two shrines locates on north of footpath, and other two on south.

Condition: Mound with brick
Description: Upper structure is completely missing. Small amount of brick fragments are the only elements confirmed on this mound.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite, some stone objects and looted hole
Description: Upper structure is completely missing. There are many accumulated brick, laterite and pebbles. There are two fragments of a sandstone pedestal which can be joined together at the southern edge of this mound. There is a looted deep hole (depth is 3.6m) at the center of mound. Some brick masonry is remaining in the hole.

Condition: Mound with brick and stone object
Description: Upper structure is completely missing. Only small amounts of brick are scattered. One sandstone element that seems to be a part of colonnette is found on this mound.

Condition: Mound with brick
Description: Upper structure is completely missing. Only a small amount of bricks are scattered.

Name: Prasat Anlong Chambok or Prasat Bos Ream
Composition: Single shrine with enclosure wall and a pond at northeast side
Condition: Remain with upper half of roof part, looted hole
Description: This brick shrine is a relatively well conserved building. Laterite enclosure wall which is surrounding this shrine (approx. 60m in east-west direction and 35m in north-south direction) and the middle point of the east side has mound which is probably buried a gate structure. One pond with laterite embankment is located at the northeast of this enclosure. Many roots entwine the shrine. Looted hole is not back filled. Plan of this shrine is rectangular, and three sides exclude the east front side have central projection, but the projection is very shallow. Typical decorative elements such as lintel and colonnette are not engraved around the false door at the projected part. This simple designing pattern is similar to M.17 (N18) and M.21 (N.22).

Name: N20 Group
Description: Parmentier described only the south shrine which is most well preserved structure. North and Central shrines were completely destroyed and remained as mounds.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and some stone elements
Description: This is an unexcavated mound with scattered bricks and small laterite blocks. Size of laterite blocks are almost same size as brick pieces. The amount of laterite block is much greater than brick.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and some stone elements
Description: This mound was disturbed by the looted hole; 2.0m depth * 1.7m NS * 2.1m EW. Some parts of brick masonry remain in the looted hole. Though this is a middle shrine of a three shrines complex, the volume of this mound is not as big as a central shrine that is supposed to be greater than two shrines beside it. Amount of laterite block is almost same as brick. A scattered pedestal is small and rectangle in shape.

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the wall and platform
Description: This is rectangular planed shrine. Upper roof structure is collapsed, but part of wall remained. This building is a different style from other brick shrines in Sambor Prei Kuk. Curving of Flying Palace, pilaster, and somasutra are of a unique design. One of the lintels (No. 352, B000L07) was excavated in front of this shrine in 2000 by SCP. A large pedestal of an interesting style is scattered in front of the shrine. This pedestal is similar style with the pedestal that was excavated at the top of Mak Pi (M.050). In 2025–2026, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk conducted excavation work and restoration/installation work on a sandstone mandapa discovered in front of the eastern side of this shrine. The mandapa was a canopy-type pedestal with four pillars, similar to the mandapa of Prasat Yeai Poeun, and was decorated with elaborate carvings.

Name: No name
Composition: Single shrine but inside the outer enclosure of Prasat Sambor
Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: This building is located on the slightly high land from the Prasat Sambor. This shrine is still conserved almost top of the roof. The elevation of this tower is of an individual style in this group of the monument. This square planed shrine faces to west direction. Bowl of the Somasutra is installed on the north wall, and two wooden boards were originally sat at the back wall and one board was sat at the left (north) wall. Trace for support the ceiling is also very unique. Holes for inserting the beams remained on the east and west wall. Some of the dangerous trees around the shrine were cut by SCP. In 2025, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk conducted trench excavations around the exterior of the building to investigate the structure of its base and its relationship with the surrounding terrain. Partial reinforcement and conservation works were also carried out.

Name: Prasat Deom Cham
Description: 3 shrines originally stood in north-south alignment, but only the central shrine is preserved today, and both structures beside it were completely collapsed. This group is located inside the outer enclosure of Prasat Sambor and positions at the symmetrical position with the N22 shrine in the complex Prasat Sambor.  In the 2020s, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk removed accumulated deposits at the site, exposed the bases of the three shrines, and carried out consolidation measures.

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: Plan of this shrine is rectangular and two steps of projection at each sides. This shrine is in sound condition. Wall and three tier of roof structure are well observed. Decoration elements around the door, lintel and colonnette, pediment is curved out from brick at three sides except for the facade. Style of these lintels is in Sambor Prei Kuk style. Although Parmentier mentioned that this shrine was constructed in the later period than the main temples of Sambor Prei Kuk, the evidence is not clear. An inscription date in 10th century, but this engraving might be later than construction work. Decoration of the lintel, Flying Palace, and so on is similar design to the shrines of N group, and the analysis of brick was also belonged into same zone of the main shrines of N group. One stone lintel was confirmed by old photograph, but this element is missing today.

Condition: Mound with brick
Description: Upper structure is completely missing, and some stone elements are scattered. The shrine was probably remaining at the beginning of 20th century, because Parmentier described on this shrine in detail.

Condition: Mound with brick
Description: Upper structure is completely missing, and some stone elements are scattered.

Name: Ashram Maha Rosei
Composition: Single shrine with terrace, inside the outer enclosure of Prasat Sambor.
Condition: Some stone elements were missing, but nearly complete remains
Description: One of the most unique sandstone buildings in this group of monument. Almost all stone elements remain, except for both colonnettes and lintel. Pedestal in the chamber is also missed, but trace of the shape of pedestal is observed on the floor. There are many bullet holes on the wall. Parmentier recorded the existence of the terrace that was confirmed by the excavation survey. Confirmed terrace is square, almost 9m each and eastern side was projected plan (Pl.25, AKP). It is a very acceptable theory that this building was covered by another bigger wooden structure. In the 2020s, with financial support from the Government of India, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk conducted excavations around the building, reconfirming the terrace structure recorded by Parmentier. A major restoration project was also carried out: all sandstone elements of the building were dismantled, conserved, and reassembled.

Name: Prasat Chrey
Composition: Single shrine with a pond at west side, inside the outer enclosure of Prasat Sambor
Condition: Remain with the top of the roof part
Description: One of the best preserved brick shrines in this group of monument. Almost all exterior decoration of the wall and upper three tiers of roof structure remain except for the eastern projected part. There is no edicule decoration on the wall, called Flying Palace, but the wall of the each upper tier has the edicule decorations. Typical decoration elements, lintel, colonnette, is not engraved around the false doors. This type of the architectural style is similar to M.21 (N22). Although M.12 (N24) might be similar style to this shrine, it is difficult to distinguish because the upper part of decoration of M.12 was largely missed. Sandstone board is installed at the back wall of interior space. It is only single example of remaining this board, though several shrines can be observed the trace of board on the interior wall. Somasutra is installed in the north wall. Roots of the tree entwine around this shrine, and making an amazing sight. However, some part of this shrine is in a very dangerous condition because roots also entwine in the interior, and invade the brick structure. Long branches were cut back several times by SCP. One pond is locates at the northwest side of the shrine.

Name: None
Composition: Single long structure, inside the outer enclosure of Prasat Sambor
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite
Description: This is a single mound that is located at the western bank of M.017’s pond. This is an unexcavated mound with laterite and small amount of bricks. Laterite pavement is confirmed at the north side of the mound. There is a bombed crater 5m away in a northwest direction.

Name: None
Condition: Remain with the lower half of the wall and plat form
Description: It is a single brick building of laterite. This shrine is isolated in the lower flooded area but it may be a related building with temple complex, Prasat Sambor. One layer of pedestal is confirmed from the eastern side of the mound. Door frame elements, sill and jamb remain in the original position. Interior wall 0.5m in height remains, and no looted hole is confirmed.

Name: Prasat Sambor
Description: One of the three most important temple complexes. This temple is surrounded by three enclosure walls, although previously it was recognized that the temple compounds were limited in two enclosure area. Outer most enclosure is square plan and each side 389m. But it is not sure when the outer most enclosure was constructed. An earthen dike, causeway for this temple, extended about 2km to the east direction from the east step structure N34.

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the wall and platform
Description: This shrine stands on the square terrace (approx. 40m in each direction). Terrace is floored by sandstone pavement. Sandstone steps are unearthed from all four cardinal directions, but north step was severely destroyed probably by human intervention. Four lines of pillar hole were found at the eastern floor. Wooden chamber may be installed in the later period only at the front direction of this shrine. All of the structure above the wall was collapsed and around the door openings are also severely damaged. This is the only single shrine that has four door openings to every direction in this group of monuments. Clearance of the accumulated brick and soil around shrine was done in 2001 as well as installing of support to each door openings. Additional brick work was confirmed at the foot of platform by this clearance. Excavation survey in the main chamber was done in 2004. Deep looted hole was found at the center, although no object was detected from the hole. As many pieces of the Yoni element were found from the soil and brick accumulation around the shrine, SCP did reconstruction of this element and sat on the brick base in 2007. Only single square Yoni plate was reconstructed. Lower pedestal layers were not found, but some stone boards that were unearthed from the accumulation around the shrine can be a part of this pedestal. Accumulated brick and soil on the central terrace was cleared and partially restored in 2009-10. North step of the central shrine was also heavily disturbed as same as the north step of terrace. Stucco decoration was partially confirmed on the platform by this clearance. N1 shrine was completed the restoration work in 2017 by SCP.

Function: South-east small shrine on the main terrace
Condition: Mound with brick
Description: It is not confirmed the existence of any structure in this small mound.

Function: South-west small shrine on the main terrace
Condition: Mound with partially wall
Description: This shrine is a set with N4 as a symmetrical location. Brick wall is partially visible.

Function: North-west small shrine on the main terrace
Condition: Remain with the lower half of the wall and platform
Description: Excavation survey by SCP in 2008. Brick work is very poor technique and condition. This shrine was additional structure at a late period, because the poor brick work and unearthed reused Lintel and Colonnette.

Function: North-east small shrine on the main terrace
Condition: Mound with brick
Description: It is not sure the existence of any structure in this small mound. Excavation survey of the corner of northeast terrace by SCP in 2005.

Function: East gate of the inner enclosure
Condition: Mound
Description: South half of gate structure was excavated by SCP in 2006.

Function: South gate of the inner enclosure
Condition: Mound
Description: Structure is completely covered by accumulated soil.

Function: West gate of the inner enclosure
Condition: Mound
Description: Parmentier excavated and recorded this structure in AKP.

Function: North gate of the inner enclosure
Condition: Mound
Description: Structure is completely covered by accumulated soil.

Condition: Remain with the top of the roof part
Description: This is a single shrine of the octagonal plan in this temple complex. 4 tiers of roof are at least remaining. Stucco and pigment partially remain on the Flying Palace. Excavation survey in the main chamber in 2003. Pillar holes were confirmed on the brick floor at corners of the pedestal. In other words, we can conjecture that the wooden canopy was installed for covering the deity on the pedestal. Looted hole was confirmed under the lowest layer of the large round pedestal, but no object was found through the excavation survey of this hole. Front step was restored in 2007. One lintel in Sambor Prei Kuk style was unearthed from the front of this shrine. Broken pedestal fragments that were collected by the excavation in the chamber were reconstructed with a small amount of new sandstone in 2007-2008. Temporary support was added at the inner opening in 2012. (All works were done by SCP)

Function: Pedestal in front of the N7 shrine
Condition: Reconstructed
Description: This pedestal was found by the excavation survey in 2008. Detected stone elements were restored and inner brick structure and stone pedestal was reconstructed in 2007-2008 by SCP. Surrounding square brick steps was constructed for supporting the soil, and this is not reconstructed as original.

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: This is a rectangular shrine. Relatively large space between door opening and the main chamber. Somasutra is installed in north wall and two sets of stone projected for supporting the wooden board at the both side of interior wall. 4 tiers of roof structure remain. Excavation survey in the main chamber and reconstruction of the pedestal in 2005 by SCP. Only the yoni plate was sat on the concrete fake pedestal, and original stone pedestal layers were laid in front of the shrine because the original stone elements are too damaged to reassemble.

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: This is a square shrine and almost same dimension as N10 shrine. 5 tiers of roof structure remain. Excavation survey was carried out in 2005 to search for the pedestal element. However, none of the pedestal fragments were found from the looted deep hole. Replica of the deity “Durga” was installed on the concrete cube base in 2006. (All works were done by SCP.)

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: This is a square shrine and almost same dimension with N9 shrine. 4 tiers of roof structure remain. Drainage projection of yoni (Snanadroni) directed to west irregularly. Installing the support to the door opening in 2005. Clearance survey in the main chamber and reconstruction of the pedestal in 2005. Pillar holes were confirmed at the corner of pediment as same as N7 shrine. Wooden canopy might be installed for covering the statue. Installing the replica of main statue “Harihara” in 2006. (All works were done by SCP.)

Condition: Remain with the low end of the wall and platform
Description: This structure was unearthed by the excavation survey in 2008.

Condition: Remain with the top of the roof part
Description:
This shrine has a square plan with two stepped projections on each side. It is the highest shrine in this temple complex, and three tiers of the roof structure remain. The western elevation is the best preserved. Decorative elements around the false doors were carved in brick, while the door frames were made of sandstone. The decorative lintel of the eastern doorway is recorded in old photographs, but this element is now missing. Supports were installed at the doorway in 2001. An excavation survey was carried out in the main chamber, and several stone pedestal elements were found in the looted hole. A set of altar elements and the lowest layer of the pedestal were reconstructed in 2005. The upper layers of the pedestal were not found. All of these works were conducted by SCP.
This shrine is one of the most hazardous structures in this temple complex. There are many cracks, and some of the Flying Palaces are inclined outward. Full-scale restoration work is urgently needed. From 2024 to 2025, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk removed accumulated deposits around the building and carried out restoration works, including the consolidation of the brick masonry.

Condition: Remain with the lower half of the roof part
Description: This shrine is square plan with two steps of projection at each side. Only first tier of the roof is partially observed and confirmed the high tier style as same as symmetrical positioned N11 shrine. Collapsed brick filled in the interior space. Decorative motif on the Flying Palace and pilaster were destroyed by flat chisel. Original design was possibly changed in the later period. This kind of the destructive modification can be confirmed only in this shrine in this temple complex.

Condition: Remain with the lower half of the roof part
Description: This shrine has a square plan with flat wall. Only first tier of the roof is partially observed. Collapsed brick filled in the interior space. Many round holes are engraved on four sides of exterior wall. Dimension of these holes are 10cm in diameter and 10cm in depth. Holes are opened three levels; middle of wall, upper part of wall, and molding above the wall. Holes are never opened on a vertical line. This kind of holes are observed only at the S1 shrine.

Function: East shrine between inner and middle enclosure
Condition: Remain with the upper half of the wall and platform
Description: This shrine has a rectangular plan and remains by the top of wall. Clearance excavation survey was conducted at the front half building and the looted hole in the chamber in 2005 by SCP. Perfect shape of lintel in Sambor Prei Kuk style was discovered in front of this mound. Looted hole was re-excavated almost 7m in depth, but no pedestal or other objects were unearthed. Brick layer was confirmed about 1.5m thickness under floor level. Restoration work was carried out in 2011 and 2012, and consolidated the loose brick masonry.

Function: North shrine between inner and middle enclosure
Condition: Remain with the lower half of the wall and platform
Description: This structure was previously covered by accumulated soil and only the door jamb was visible. Clearance excavation survey was carried out in 2005 and confirmed the square plan of shrine. But the brick structure was heavily damaged and quality of original brick work is possibly poorer than the other shrines in this temple complex. A set of beautiful pedestal, yoni, and linga was unearthed from the mound. SCP conducted the restoration work of this pedestal with partial new sandstone in 2005. Stone of linga is fine grain sandstone, not typical sandstone. Original linga stone was stored in storehouse and concrete linga was installed at the site.

Function: East gate of the middle enclosure
Condition: Remain with the lower half of the wall and platform
Description: Excavation survey was carried out on and nearby this gate in 2006 by SCP.

Function: South gate of the middle enclosure
Condition: Mound with laterite blocks
Description: Door jambs and collapsed laterite structure.

Function: West gate of the middle enclosure
Condition: Mound with laterite blocks
Description: Door jambs and collapsed laterite structure.

Function: North gate of the middle enclosure
Condition: Mound with laterite blocks
Description: No laterite structure was observed above ground.

Condition: Stepped stones were paved
Description: South half of this stepped terrace was excavated in 2006 by SCP. Important Nak-Ta shrine for the villager around this area is standing beside this terrace.

Condition: Small mound with some stone elements
Description: Sandstone block of door frame are scattered.

Condition: Mound with brick and some stone elements
Description: Broken moonstone is scattered.

Condition: No trace but estimate the existence
Description: None

Name: No name
Composition: Single shrine but inside the outer enclosure of Prasat Sambor
Condition: Remain with the top of the roof part
Description: This shrine may stands on the elevated terrace. Laterite lined structure partially appeared at the edge of the south terrace and some columns, laterite blocks are scattered at the east side. This shrine is a rectangular plan with two steps projection and remains up to three tiers roof. No space between door opening and main chamber. Somasutra penetrates the north wall. Decorative elements around false door; lintel and colonnette, is not represented as typical composition. Edicule decorations are situated on the upper roof layers whereas no Flying Palace on the wall. These above architectural styles are similar to N22 shrine. East lintel is unique style (“type 2” by Parmentier classification). Excavation survey was carried out in the main chamber in 2005. Then unearthed pedestal was restored and reset there. The replica of main statue “Brahma” was installed on the pedestal in 2006. Accumulation soil around the platform was removed in 2006. (All works were done by SCP.)

Name: No name
Condition: Remain with the upper half of the wall
Description: This shrine is a rectangular plan. Roof and upper part of wall was collapsed and the platform was covered by collapse brick. Flying palace was engraved on the wall. Excavation trenches by the G, Groslier in 1960s remain around this shrine.

Name: No name
Composition: Single shrine but possibly belongs to Prasat Yeai Poeun
Condition: Remain with the platform and steps
Description: Lowest part of the platform and steps in front of the door opening remains. A large pond that is embanked by laterite block is located in front of this shrine.

Name: Prasat Yeai Poeun
Description: One of the three most important temple complexes. This temple is surrounded by two enclosure walls. An earthen dike, causeway for this temple, extended east direction from the east gate to the Kampong Chhuteal village about 2.5km in length.

Condition: Remain with the top of the roof part
Description: Several old photographs recorded the work of soil clearance and tree cutting works surrounding this shrine in 1927. Pedestal in the chamber was also assembled in the same year, but the pedestal was destroyed again during the conflict period in 1970s to 1980s. This shrine stands on the rectangular terrace at 1.5m height. Terrace is floored by sandstone pavement and sandstone steps at four coordinal directions. West step and moonstone of the terrace are largely disturbed. This is one of the largest structures in this group of monuments. Total height of this shrine from the top level of the terrace is approximately 21m. Another high shrine, the central shrine of Prasat Tao is almost same height at present condition. Three tiers of roof part remained with fruitful decoration. Plan of this shrine is rectangular with two projections at each side. East-West dimension is approx. 15m, and north-south is 12m. Interior space is 9m east-west and 5m north-south. Interior wall is also decorated pilasters. Only S2 and C1 shrines have interior pilaster except for this shrine. Style of the lintel is unique and not a typical Sambor Prei Kuk style. Faces of several figures in the east lintel were destroyed the past half century while old photograph represent these faces. West lintel was transferred to the National museum in Phnom Penh. Decorative surface at the Flying Palace and pediment are destroyed and changed the design by stucco in later period, or applied the stucco motif since the initial construction time. Figures in each edicule decoration at the upper tier remain. Small holes are observed on the wall the same as N13 shrine, but the function is unknown. Holes are aligned at three levels. Worshippers have to climb up the steps and pass the entrance door and again declined the steps and moonstone to enter the main chamber. This kind of largely up and down access is one of the features of some shrines in this temple complex. Excavation survey of the looted hole was carried out in 2004. Then, unearthed pedestal fragments were assembled at the center of main chamber. Because two more unidentified pedestal layers were left, we set them behind the assembled central pedestal. In 2024, restoration works were carried out by the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk. Along with the restoration investigation, excavations were conducted around the base of the building, and consolidation work was also carried out on the exposed terrace remains.

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: This shrine is rectangular plan and open two directions to east and west. Exterior brick is heavy damaged and composition of the elevation is not identified. Sandstone pedestal with pillars and canopy, called Manadapa, is a masterpiece of the Pre-Angkor artistry, and is installed in this shrine. Excavation survey under the Mandapa pedestal was conducted in 2004, because looted hole was opened there. Unfortunately, west half of this shrine collapsed in August 2006. Mandapa pedestal also severely damaged under the fallen brick roof. Restoration of the stone pedestal and install the temporary supports to the brick structure was conducted by Ministry of Culture and Fine Arts in 2007. Then, permanent support and roofing structure was installed in 2012 by Ministry of Culture and Fine Arts. In 2024, repair works were carried out by the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk. As part of these works, the former protective shelter covering the entire building was removed and replaced with a new protective structure covering only the interior of the shrine.

Function: East gate of the inner enclosure
Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: A looted hole dug from the eastern side of this shrine was documented by the Ministry of Culture and Fine Arts in the 1990s. Branches were cut by SCP in 2003. In the early 2020s, repair works were carried out by the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk, and the trees entangled with the building were removed.

Function: South gate of the inner enclosure
Condition: Remain with the upper half of the wall and platform
Description: Same plan as east gate S3 but the outer (south) door is closed by brick work and this gate was closed to worshippers. Cutting the dangerous tree nearby shrine in 2005 by SCP.

Function: West gate of the inner enclosure
Condition: Remain with the lower half of the wall and platform
Description: Round decorations (medallion) are well preserved on the enclosure wall beside this gate.

Function: North gate of the inner enclosure
Condition: Remain with the upper half of the wall but covered by the accumulated brick
Description: It is probably that the outer door was closed the same as the south gate, though the door is visible due to the collapsed brick mound.

Condition: Remain with the top of the roof part
Description: This shrine is an octagonal plan. Three tiers of roof part remain. Figure motif in Flying Palace was not destroyed. No pedestal elements were found in the chamber. Brick wall was previously installed for supporting the interior lintel, and accumulation soil around the shrine was removed by EFEO. Platform of a rectangular building with stone steps at the west side was unearthed by the past excavation survey in 1920s. This structure is again covered by soil and not visible today. Installation of the temporary tie support in 2006 by SCP. One of the old photos shows the very well preserved and sophisticated lintel probably from S7 shrine.

Condition: Remain with the top of the roof part

Several holes are observed on the east projected pilaster probably for supporting the beam of wooden front chamber in the later period. S11 is the other shrine which remains the pilaster beside the door opening and the symmetrical positioned building, but no hole was observed. Temporary tie support was Installed in 2006 by SCP. Interior floor was cast by concrete in 2007 by Ministry of Culture and Fine Arts.

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part

Pedestal was perfectly reassembled by four stone layers including one yoni plate with drainage projection (Snanadroni). But no deity image was found.

Name: Prasat Tao
Description: One of the three most important temple complexes. This temple is surrounded by two enclosure walls. There are no north and south gates at both enclosures. One rectangular pond with laterite embankment is located at the northeast corner in the outer enclosure. No inscriptions or sculptures have been discovered at Prasat Tao, and no excavation surveys have been conducted in the past. Therefore, there are only weak grounds for dating the C1 shrine, but it is generally thought to have been built in the 9th century, during the reign of Jayavarman II, who founded the Angkor Empire. This is because the decorative style of the decorative lintels and colonnettes in the central shrine is that of the Kampong Preah style, which appeared in the later years of the pre-Angkor period, and because the sculptural style of lion images protecting the openings resembles the lion images in Phnom Kulen, where the royal capital is said to have been located in the 9th century (Stern, 1938; Remusat, 1940).

Condition: Remain with the top of the roof part
Description: This is the only single well preserved structure that still stands in sound condition in this temple complex. This shrine may stand on the elevated terrace, but no brick structure and stone step are presently confirmed. Plan of this shrine is rectangular with one projection for each side. Dimension of plan is smaller than S1 shrine, and E-W direction is approx. 15m and N-S direction is approx. 12m. Height of this tower is about 21m almost same as S1 shrine, but both of the upper most structure are collapsed and original height is not identified. Three tiers of roof structure remained. Originally a set of lion statues stood on the side stone of steps for three sides except for west step. We can confirm these lions only on the east side today. Among the decorative lintels, the lintel on the east side of the front face is rough and appears incomplete. However, as careful observation reveals a number of small holes in the lintel at regular intervals, metallic decorative pieces had probably been inserted in the small holes to embellish the surface of the stone element. No other examples like this have been found at the Angkor monuments. Style of the decorative lintels and colonnettes at the other sides is that of the Kampong Preah style. The brick masonry displays a technique whereby a sandstone plate is inserted below the Flying Palace and other extended tiers, as well as variations in masonry technique that are more sophisticated than other temples. There are no detailed decorations in the Pediment, Pilaster and Flying Palace, even in the edicules at upper layers. Finishing stucco may have been applied for molding the decorative motif. There are only fragments of small pedestal in the chamber. Pilaster are regularly projected on interior wall as same as S1 and S2 shrines. Ministry of Culture and Fine Arts carried out the restoration works; filling the looted hole, installation of RC support for east door opening, and reconstruct the side wall of east steps and lions. From 2022 to 2023, investigations of the interior were carried out by the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk. These investigations revealed the presence of a large square subterranean chamber-like space beneath the floor. In addition, restoration works were conducted up to 2025. As part of these works, excavations were carried out around the northern step area of the building, revealing terrace remains that raised the shrine one level above the surrounding ground. Conservation and repair works were also carried out, including treatment of the exposed terrace remains.

Name: Trapeang Ropeak
Description: This temple complex and M.40 are called the Trapeang Ropeak group. The central shrine of this complex faces west. One of the four subsidiary shrines at the corners, the northwest shrine (Z3), remains and faces south. Although the other subsidiary shrines have completely collapsed and no structures have been confirmed, the southwest shrine (Z2), which formed a pair with Z3, can be estimated to have faced north. From 2024 to 2025, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk removed accumulated deposits around the site and carried out repair works, including the consolidation of brick masonry.

Condition: Remain with the top of the roof part
Description: Well preserved shrine. Plan of this shrine is square with two steps projection at each side. Four tiers of roof remain, and total height is approx. 17m in present condition. One of the lintel element is confirmed at the original position in an old photograph, but all of the sandstone elements not only lintel but also doorframe are now missing. One lintel that was retained at the site storage was transferred and exhibited in the National Museum at Phnom Penh. Finishing stucco remained well on the wall, in particular on the Flying Palace. Unfortunately, one of the most beautiful of stucco decoration, celestial lady, was stolen in 2007.

Condition: Mound with brick
Description: This is an unexcavated mound. Brick masonry is partially confirmed on the mound at the east side.

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the wall and platform
Description: This building has an octagonal plan and faces south. The western corner was destroyed by the collapse of a large tree in 2002. From 2024 to 2025, restoration work was carried out by the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk.

Condition: Mound with brick and some stone elements
Description: This is an unexcavated mound. Partial brick masonry remains at the west side of the mound. One layer of square stone pedestal is scattered on the mound.

Condition: Mound with brick and some stone elements
Description: This is an unexcavated mound. Partial brick masonry remains at the south side. One layer of square stone pedestal is scattered on the mound.

Name: None
Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: This shrine has an octagonal plan and faces east. All stone elements around the doorway are missing, as in the M.039 (Z1) shrine. Small square holes are regularly arranged on the three main walls, and the brick surfaces around them were chipped, probably for the installation of decorative panels. Large tree roots entwine the shrine, and long branches were cut several times by SCP. In 2025, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk carried out large-scale removal of trees from the upper part of the shrine and proceeded with clearance work to remove accumulated deposits around the building.

Name: Prasat Kouktroung
Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: This shrine has a rectangular plan and faces east. The roof remains up to the second false story. The elements around the doorway, including the lintel and colonnettes, are carved from the brick wall. The southern lintel is well preserved and retains its original design in the Sambor Prei Kuk style. The motifs of the Flying Palaces have not been destroyed, making this a very rare shrine without destructive ancient renovation, similar to Prasat Tamon (M.061). This shrine remains in relatively sound condition, and dangerous trees with roots entwining the shrine were removed in 2004. However, many parts of the roof and doorway are in an extremely hazardous condition, and immediate supports should be installed at each unstable part. An aerial LiDAR survey identified traces of what appears to be a fairly large east–west elongated enclosure wall centered on this tower.

Name: Prasat Kong Veang

Description: This is a small mound with scattered brick, laterite and pebbles. Partially brick masonry (partially mixed with laterite) remains inside the hole. This hole kept the nearly original square shape (1.9m x 1.9m).

Name: No name
Composition: Central shrine and some structures are located in the brick enclosure wall.
Condition: A part of looted central shrine and some parts of enclosure walls appear above ground level.
Dimension: NS: 58.3m, EW: Unknown but more than 60 (m)
Description:
This site is located on the road to M.051 and M.052. During the construction of a detour, SCP excavated seven trenches at the complex, from M45-A to M45-G, in 2007. The enclosure wall measures approximately 60 m square. Part of the central shrine and some sections of the enclosure wall are visible above ground. The central brick shrine had been looted, and an original square shaft beneath the pavement level, measuring approximately 1.7 m square, is visible. Excavations revealed a brick enclosure wall and a laterite column structure with a laterite moonstone.
The site is located on the western bank of the O Krou Ke River. A large earthen embankment extends eastward from the site. This embankment is interpreted as a dam constructed to block the O Krou Ke River, suggesting a close relationship between the dam and this temple.

Condition: Mound with brick and looted hole
Description: This area was excavated in 2001 to collect soil for the construction of a new road in Sambor village. During this soil collection, many ceramics, bricks, and laterite fragments were found. SCP conducted an excavation survey in 2007 within the depression created by the soil collection. The excavation revealed deposits containing various artifacts and materials extending to a depth of more than 2 m. In some areas, remains that may have been a brick floor were also identified.

Name: Mak Pi
Condition: Big mound with many stone elements
Description: The mound is located on the west bank of O Krou Ke. There are many brick, laterite and sandstone objects on and around a mound. At the center of the mound, one big pedestal was found under the surface soil (similar type as MUNDAPA pedestal at S2 shrine). Villagers said that many stone object in this place were transported from the other place and it is impossible to identify the provenance. Except for this mound, laterite pavement structure is also confirmed near the river bank. In 2014, local market and reception center were constructed at the south side of the Mak Pi mound.

Name: Srei Krup Leak
Composition: 7 structures are concentrated. Probably two different complexes and one shrine are closely located (L1-L3, L4 and L5-L7).

Function: Library of the group with L1-L3 (?)
Condition: Remains with the lower half of the wall
Description: This small brick structure is heavily damaged. Although it is seems to be Library structure, direction of door opening is east.

Function: Gate of the group with L1-L3 (?)
Condition: Remains with the lower half of the wall
Description: Although this structure is located in front of L3 structure and seems to be gate structure, it is uncertain the function due to very poor preserved condition.

Function: Main shrine of the group with L1-L3 (?)
Condition: Remains with the upper half of the wall and platform
Description: This structure is located nearby L1 and L2, and largest dimension. This rectangular structure may open to west direction.

Condition: Remains with the upper half of the roof part
Description: This small shrine is located between the L1–L3 group and the L5–L7 group. It has a square plan with projections on each side and faces east. Many small holes are carved into the brick surface, probably for attaching decorative plaster. A deep looted hole opens inside the shrine. In the early 2020s, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk conducted excavations around the building. Restoration works were also carried out, including the consolidation of the brick masonry and the reconstruction and installation of the pedestal inside the shrine.

Function: Main shrine of the group with L5-L7 (?)
Condition: Remains with the upper half of the roof part
Description: This shrine consists of a main chamber and an antechamber. The main chamber represents an early style of the pre-Angkorian period. The roof is of a low-story type and is decorated with kudus. The oldest inscription date in this group of monuments is recorded on this door jamb; however, the door jamb is a reused element. The main chamber was probably constructed in the pre-Angkorian period, while the attached chamber was added in a later period. In the early 2020s, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk conducted clearance investigations to remove accumulated deposits around the L5–L7 group, and restoration works were also carried out. These investigations also revealed the existence of an enclosure wall surrounding the entire group.

Function: Library of the group with L5-L7 (?)

Description: This structure is estimated to be library, because it faces to the west and is located at the southeast of main shrine L5.

Function: Gate of the group with L5-L7 (?)

Description: This structure is positioned in front of the main shrine L5 and opened two directions, east and west. One curious lintel was discovered near this shrine in 2005.

An antechamber was attached in a later period. The lintel is in the pre-Angkorian style, specifically the Prei Khmeng style, but both sides were cut, indicating that it was clearly a reused element. The main chamber has a square plan without projections on any side. 
In 2015, SCP conducted excavations and identified repair remains around the K6 shrine. Similar repair remains were also confirmed at the K7 shrine. In addition, from 2024 to 2025, the National Authority for Sambor Prei Kuk carried out clearance investigations around the building and implemented repair measures.
