Stand_in: Forest, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This mound is covered by collapsed brick. Vertical pit that was probably caused by the looting is confirmed in the chamber. Dimension of the hole is in 3.4m (N-S), 4m (E-W), and 2.5m in depth. There are two ponds at the northwest side and north side of the moat of Isanapura city close to the northern side.

Name: Prasat Tamon, Prasat Rosei Roliek
Composition: Main shrine and sub shrine at the northwest direction

Condition: Remains with the top of the roof part
Description: Partition wall between main chamber and antechamber is relatively larger than the other shrines. Interior is heavy damaged by the previous looting. Large vertical cracks are opened in the wall and upper structure is in a highly hazardous condition. Decorative elements around the false door opening are engraved from brick body. Only the upper door flame is made of sandstone at the each false door. Flying Palace and lintel decoration in Sambor Prei Kuk style are very well conserved on the exterior. Motives of Flying Palace have never been destroyed the same as M.041 (W).

Condition: Mound
Description: Upper structure is completely destroyed.

Name: No Name
Condition: Mound with partially remained wall and looted hole
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This mound is covered by collapsed brick. Partially remaining interior wall and brick structure is confirmed in the looted hole 3m in depth. This shrine opens to the east and sandstone drainage element “Somasutra” remains on the north wall.

Name: No Name
Condition: Mound with partially remained wall and looted hole
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This mound is covered by collapsed brick and pebbles that might have originated from the looted hole. Under the floor at 2.4m in depth, the looted hole appeared to be the original vertical hole.

Name: No Name
Condition: Scattering bricks are concentrated
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: Half part of this mound was destroyed during the construction work of extending the road in 2002. Present day, only some scattered bricks are confirmed.

Name: No Name
Composition: Single shrine but very close from M.066 group
Condition: Mound with partially remaining wall
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This mound is covered by collapsed brick. Interior wall can be observed partially, and an octagonal plan can be confirmed. Looted hole under the floor in the main chamber is 1.75 in depth. One pond is located at the east side of the shrine.

Name: No name
Composition: Three shrines in north south alignment and enclosed by earthen rampart. Pond of laterite embankment is located at north east direction.
Description: Brick gate structure with steps and moonstone was excavated under the earthen rampart at the east side of central shrine by the excavation survey in 2014.

Condition: Mound with interior wall
Description: Brick wall is confirmed inside the mound about 2.5m in depth. Although it is uncertain if the wall is the wall above the floor or under the floor. It is highly probable that this wall is the wall of the basement.

Condition: Mound with interior wall
Description: Brick wall is confirmed inside the mound about 2.6m in depth and 1.8m (N-S) x 1.5m (E-W). Identical to the southern shrine, this interior wall is highly probable to be that of the basement wall. The wall is randomly piled brick and laterite and beneath this layer directly continued to the foundation by compacted soil and laterite.

Condition: Mound
Description: This building is severely damaged and confirmed only a small mound with scattering bricks.

Name: No Name
Composition: This structure probably belong with M.066 group
Condition: Mound with looted hole
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This mound is severely damaged. Looted hole is 1.7m in depth.

Name: No Name
Condition: Mound with looted hole
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This mound is completely collapsed. Looted hole opens the center of the mound. Broken drainage sandstone “Somasutra” are scattered on the mound.

Name: No Name
Condition: Mound with partial wall
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This is a laterite shrine. Looted hole appears at the central pit with 3.2m depth, 1.5m (N-S) x 1.3m (E-W). Central pit also constructed of the laterite elements. One pond is located at the eastern side. About 300m earthen rampant extends from this shrine to the east side. It seems like a causeway. Newly constructed road and extension of the rice field are destroying the earthen structure.

Name: Donmong
Condition: Mound with partial wall
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This mound is covered by the collapsed bricks. The wall of central pit under the floor appears looted at 1.4m depth, 2.85m (N-S) and 2.76m (E-W). One part of the drainage stone “Somasutra” remains at the north wall, and some other parts of this element are scattered on the mound.

Name: Donmong
Composition: Two shrines are aligned in north-south direction. Small hole is opened between them.

Condition: Mound with partial brick wall and looted hole
Description: Looted hole is open at the center of the mound approximately 1.3m in depth.

Condition: Mound with partial brick wall and looted hole
Description: This mound is covered by collapsed bricks. Looted hole is open at the center of the mound approximately 2.7m in depth and brick walls are confirmed in the hole.

Name: Donmong
Composition: Two shrines are aligned in north-south direction. One pond embanked with laterite is located at the northeast side.

Condition: Mound with partial brick wall and looted hole
Description: The mound is covered by collapsed brick and soil. Looted hole is open at the center of the mound approximately 1.3m in depth and 1.8m (N-S), 2.3m (E-W).

Condition: Mound with partial brick wall and looted hole
Description: The mound is covered by collapsed brick and soil. A vertical pit is observed at the center of the mound approximately 2.7m in depth and 1.2m (N-S), 1.4m (E-W) in the looted hole.

Name: Donmong
Condition: Scattering brick and laterite with looted hole
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: Small amount of scattered brick and laterite are concentrated around the looted hole.

Name: No name
Condition: Many bricks are scattered.
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This site is located at the eastern side of the M.075 group. Probably this is a form of causeway for M.075 group. Many bricks are scattered, and the existence and shape of the structure is unclear.

Name: Donmong
Composition: Four shrines are located in the enclosure wall. Three of them are aligned in east-west direction. Moreover, one structure is on the south-east side from central shrine.
Description: Four shrines are located within the enclosure wall. Three of them are aligned in an east–west direction, and another structure is located to the southeast of the central shrine. SCP conducted excavation work to remove accumulated deposits at the eastern gate of this temple. Excavations were also carried out at the northeastern corner of the enclosure wall. The investigations suggested that the eastern gate had undergone several phases of modification.

Condition: Mound with partial interior wall and door frame
Description: The mound is covered by collapsed brick and soil. Stone door frame remains at the original position in the mound.

Condition: Remain with the upper half of the roof part
Description: Plan is rectangular and projected only on the east side. Trace of the drainage stone “Somasutra” remains on the north wall.

Condition: Mound
Description: This structure is severely damaged.

Condition: Mound
Description: This structure is severely damaged.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with partial wall
Stand_in: Bush, within the moat of ancient city
Description: This mound is completely collapsed and covered by accumulated brick. Brick interior wall above floor and wall of vertical pit are confirmed in the looted hole.

Name: Russey
Condition: Mound with partial wall
Stand_in: Villager’s compound, within the moat of ancient city
Description: The mound is covered by accumulated laterite and soil. Laterite wall is partially confirmed. One monolith sandstone pedestal was found. This shrine is nearly behind (west side) the M. 075 group and intersecting point of the two earthen ramparts that are across the O Krou Ke river from Prasat Sambor and Prasat Yeai Poeun. Therefore, this shrine might be an important place for connecting the temple area and city area.

Name: Proeng Touch
Composition: Three brick structures are visible above ground in the northern part of the enclosure. In the central area within the enclosure, subsurface brick remains have been identified through archaeological excavations. Gate-related remains have also been confirmed at the center of the northern side and on the southern part of the western side of the enclosure.
Description: This site is enclosed by a rectangular perimeter wall measuring approximately 130 m east–west and 160 m north–south. Three brick structures are visible above ground, and several additional brick remains have been identified in the central area of the site through archaeological excavations. Brick gates have been confirmed at the center of the northern side of the enclosure wall and at a position slightly south of the center of the western side. A large pond with laterite revetments is located in the northeastern corner inside the enclosure. Excavations and GPR surveys were conducted in 2012 and 2026. Trench excavations at the centers of the eastern and southern sides of the enclosure confirmed that no gates existed at these locations.

Condition: Mound with partial brick interior wall and looted hole
Description: This mound is covered by collapsed brick and laterite fragments. Looted hole is 2m in depth, and some part of the brick structure is confirmed.

Condition: Excavated the partial brick wall and front steps
Description: Excavations confirmed that the building was a square brick shrine. Parts of the interior walls and the remains extending from the eastern frontal staircase to the doorway are preserved. The interior had been looted. A cylindrical sandstone colonnette was discovered beside the staircase in front of the building, suggesting that this structure dates to the pre-Angkorian period.

Condition: Mound with partial brick wall and looted hole
Description: A mound located in the northeastern corner of the enclosure, to the west of the pond. The center of the mound is slightly depressed, suggesting that it may have been looted in the past.

Condition: Brick gate structure was excavated
Description: This is a brick gate structure opening to the north and south. Immediately to the north of this gate, it connects with the main east–west avenue running through the center of the ancient city. Excavations have confirmed that there were no gates at the centers of the eastern and southern sides of the enclosure wall. The center of the western side lies directly beneath the road currently in use and has not yet been investigated. However, a brick gate structure allowing passage was identified at a point slightly south of the center of the western side.

Condition: Excavated brick structures
Description:
Ground Penetration Radar surveys suggested the presence of several subsurface remains with rectangular plans in the central area within the enclosure. Through two excavation campaigns conducted in 2012 and 2026, a large brick structure was identified at a depth of nearly 1 m at the exact center of the enclosure. Intermittent brick remains were also confirmed to the north of this structure.
The bricks appear to have been reused, and the construction is relatively crude compared with the more carefully built brick shrines visible above ground within the same enclosure. This suggests that the structure may belong to a later period. Future archaeological excavations in the central area and other parts of the enclosure are desirable.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with small amount of brick fragment and many laterite blocks.
Stand_in: Bush (located on the land of Mr. Hing and Phat)
Description: This is a laterite structure. One of the small pieces of pre-Angkor style pedestal is scattered on this mound. Direction of the door opening is east, and both sides of the entrance wall remain at 0.75m in height. There is one hole at the center sized at 2.45m (N-S) x 2.9m (E-W) and 3m depth. This shrine is located in the south of M.075 (biggest Donmong group).

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with small amount of brick and laterite fragments.
Stand_in: Bush (located on the land of Mr. Lee and Mrs. Viech)
Description: Heavily damaged mound. Some parts of the brick structure are confirmed in the looted central hole.

Name: Prasat Proeng
Composition: Two brick remnants are aligned in north-south direction. (Located on the land of Mr. Pach and Mrs. Yar.)

Condition: Mound with brick and laterite fragments
Description: Heavily damaged structure. Half of the structure was destroyed by the construction of a new road.

Condition: Mound with scattered brick and laterite fragments.
Description: Heavily damaged structure. Half of the structure was destroyed by the newly constructed road.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with small amount of brick and laterite fragments.
Description: Mound is covered by brick and accumulated soil. Looted hole remains at the center sized at 2.5 (NS) x 2.6 (EW) and 0.57m in depth. Brick wall is partially confirmed in the hole. Left half of the stone lintel that is obviously Sambor Prei Kuk style was found at the east side of mound. In addition, another sandstone element was also found near the root of a tree.

Name: Roung
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite fragments.
Description: Mound is covered by brick and laterite. Looted hole is opened at the center 1.7m in depth. One sandstone door frame element is scattered near the mound. Villager plant vegetables around this structure.

Name: Roung
Composition: Single shrine but very close with M.084
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite fragments.
Description: Mound is covered by brick and laterite. Looted hole is opened at the center 2.1m in depth. Brick wall is partially confirmed in the hole.

Name: No name
Condition: Low mound with small amount of brick, laterite fragments and pebbles.
Description: The mound might not have been looted. We could find some sandstone elements; one colonnette that is a cylindrical column (diameter 22cm) with single tie band and Pre-Angkor style, a part of moonstone, and some unknown function of other elements. North side of the shrine also exist another brick accumulation, but it’s not conclusive that this is the remnant of the brick structure or not.

Name: No name
Condition: Completely collapse and some bricks are collected in the hole. Half of the mound was destroyed by the recent planting intervention.
Description: Some brick and laterite fragments are concentrated in the small hole at the center of low mound. One moonstone was found in this hole.

Name: Gnep Sheet
Composition: Three brick shrines are aligned in north-south direction. (Located in the land of Mr. Gnep and Mrs. Sheet, this mound name is derived from simply land owner name)

Condition: Mound with brick and laterite fragments
Description: Exterior is completely collapsed, but interior wall partially remains. Central pit also confirmed by the result of looted hole which is 1.3m in depth.

Condition: Some part of the lower wall remain. Best conserved condition of these three shrines.
Description: Interior wall is confirmed, size of interior is 4.5m (N-S) x 3.3m (E-W). One pedestal was found in the room and drainage stone “Somasutra” still remains in the northern wall. One moonstone is scattered at the east side of the mound. Central pit was looted and confirmed the original structure; beneath the floor level is mixed usage of brick structure at the upper part and laterite structure at the lower part.

Condition: Mound with scatter brick and laterite fragments
Description: Some part of the interior wall and pit was confirmed in the looted hole.

Name: Prasat Proeng
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite fragments
Description: One brick shrine is located at the slightly rear side of the square shape of the moat. This moat has a causeway like road only on the east side, and the other three sides are completely closed. Mound was looted and opened a hole in size of 4.5m (E-W) x 2.5m (N-S) and 1.2m deep. Two pedestals were found. One of them was found in the moat and curiously two tenon holes are opened.

Name: Proeng Thom / Shroeng
Composition: An elongated mound containing a laterite terrace and several brick bases on its upper surface
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite
Description: This is a north–south elongated oval mound located slightly south of the center of the ancient city. Excavations revealed a laterite terrace structure, approximately 2 m high, with an associated drainage channel within the mound. On the southern side, the terrace was found to be connected to remains containing a long north–south interior space. Regularly arranged brick bases were identified on the upper surface of the terrace, and these are interpreted as bases that may have supported wooden columns.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite, and looted hole
Description: The mound is covered by collapsed brick and laterite. There is a looted hole at the center of the mound. Laterite structure is confirmed at the lower end of this hole. Function of a sandstone element that remains on the mound requires investigation.

Name: Thakas
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite, and looted hole
Description: The mound is covered by collapsed brick and laterite. Some pieces of the scattered laterite blocks are very large. Partial interior wall remains. Moreover, a thin layer (about 1cm thickness) of stucco remains on this wall. Some stone objects are scattered around the mound. One piece of door sill is scattered on the east side of the mound. In addition, one piece of an octagonal shape of the colonnette has unfinished engraving.

Name: No name
Composition: Three brick shrines are aligned in north-south direction.

Condition: Only lower part of interior wall remains.
Description: The mound is covered by collapsed brick and laterite. Interior wall remains, but it is not sure that this structure is the ordinal interior wall or interior wall under the floor. Width of the interior wall is 2.5m. Lower part of this wall was stacked by laterite. On the other hand, upper layer was by brick. It might be the boundary between them corresponds to the floor level. One of the sandstone pedestals is of the style of Pre-Angkor.

Condition: Only lower part of interior wall remains.
Description: The mound is covered by collapsed brick and laterite. Interior wall is only conserved, but the situation is same as south shrine. Brick and laterite are used together. This interior is smaller than south shrine, 2m x 2m, and 2m deep. Contrary to the south shrine, upper layer is stacked by brick and lower layer by laterite.

Condition: Mound with scattered laterite and hole
Description: The mound is confirmed to be only laterite blocks. Partially remaining central hole or interior space is 2m (N-S) x 2.5m (E-W) and 1m deep.

Name: No name
Composition: Three brick shrines are aligned in north-south direction.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: The mound is in relatively better condition than central and north mound. However, original surface was completely destroyed. This group of shrines are surrounded by cultivated area and highly dangerous situation by the further field extension.

Condition: Only small mound and central depression remains
Description: Only scattered bricks and trace of looted hole remain at the center.

Condition: Only small mound and central depression remain
Description: Only scattered bricks, small amount of laterites, and trace of looted hole remains at the center.

Name: Kath
Condition: Only very small mound
Description: This is only a small mound. No brick and laterite is visible.

Name: Kath
Composition: Small mound and depression with brick and laterite
Condition: Agricultural field
Dimension: Small mound and depression with brick and laterite
Description: This is only a small mound with a trace of a looted hole. Small amount of brick and laterite are scattered. This trace is surrounded by rice fields, therefore, it is dangerous situation and potentially destroyed by the extending the field. The land owner said that they found this remnant in 2002, and also some gold pieces. However, there is no confirmation of any artifacts from land owner and site.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite
Description: Brick and laterite blocks are scattered around the mound. Traces of looted hole are confirmed at the central chamber and front of this shrine. A brick wall partially remains at the southeast corner in 1m in height. There is one depression that is supposed to be an artificial pond located at northeast direction of the mound.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite
Description: Two traces of looted hole at the center and eastern side of mound are confirmed. Partial brick wall remains inside the central looted hole. One sandstone step element in front of the door remains in the original position. One rectangular pond belongs with this shrine at north side.

Name: Trapang Srortei
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: The mound is covered by collapsed brick, laterite and pebbles. Several sandstone objects are scattered around the mound. The most important element is the half part of a lintel of Prei Khmeng style. Whether this interior wall is above or under the floor level is unknown. However, this wall is the alternatively constructed of brick and laterite. Villager informed us that one statue of Vajimukha was found in this mound in the 1980s.

Name: Sratech
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Superstructure is completely collapsed, but in the looted hole appears the wall of the central pit. Dimension of the pit is 1,8m x 2.3m and 1m deep. Lower layer of the pit was constructed of laterite and upper layer was of brick. Two moonstone elements were found at this mound.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Superstructure is completely collapsed, but in the looted hole appears the wall of the central pit. Dimension of this pit is 3.3m (N-S) x 3.1m (E-W) and 1.3m deep. This wall is stacked alternately by using brick and laterite.

Name: Boeng Doemampil
Condition: Small depression of the trace of looted hole
Description: Only a small depression remains. Laterite fragments are scattered around this remnant. There are two rectangular ponds nearby this remnant at east and southwest.

Name: Khnach Tol
Composition: Three shrines are aligned in NS direction. Another shrine is located in eastern side alone.
Description: This group is one of the largest temple complexes within the ancient city. At least four brick shrines have been identified, three of which are aligned north–south. The northernmost shrine has an octagonal plan, while the other two have rectangular plans. A square pond is located in the northeastern corner inside the enclosure. Excavations have identified gate remains of different types on the northern and southern sides of the enclosure wall. Two shrines within the enclosure have been excavated and subsequently conserved.

Condition: Lower part of interior wall remains.
Description: This shrine has an octagonal plan. An excavated pit remains at the center of the interior, indicating the presence of a square vertical shaft beneath the floor. A circular sandstone pedestal was installed inside the shrine. Excavation and conservation work have been carried out by NASPK.

Condition: Exterior is covered by collapsed brick, but Interior wall remains.
Description: Interior wall remains at 1.1m in height. There are many stone elements remaining in the original place and also scattered in/around the shrine. Especially door opening elements, door frame, step, and moonstone, remain well preserved. Water drainage “Somasutra” exists on the north wall. Excavation and conservation work have been carried out by NASPK.

Condition: Exterior is covered by collapsed brick, but Interior wall partially remains.
Description: Interior wall remains 2.7m in height. Some of the sandstone elements are scattered around the mound.

Condition: Small mound with the looted trace
Description: The structure is completely destroyed. Deformed brick pavement and wall is confirmed partially in the looted trace.

Name: No name
Composition: This group consists of 6 mounds. All of the mounds are aligned in one line on north-south axis.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: The brick structure is completely destroyed. There is one big looted hole at the center and small part at the north and south corner is the remains of the brick pavement. Many sandstone elements such as door frame, moonstone, and pedestal are scattered around the mound.

Condition: Mound with brick and laterite
Description: This brick structure is completely destroyed. Some stone elements were found.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Looted hole is opened at the center of the mound, and some part of the brick structure is confirmed in the hole.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Looted hole is opened at the center of this mound, and two layers of laterite pavement are confirmed at the northeast corner. Laterite exterior platform confirmed partially at the northeast corner.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Deformed brick structure is confirmed in the hole.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Mound is covered by accumulated brick and laterite. The central hole is 2.4m in depth. The wall of the hole is piled up with laterite block and some part is by brick chips with soil. One layer of the sandstone pedestal is scattered on the mound.

Name: No name
Composition: Two shrines are aligned in north-south direction. Surrounding of this group is slightly depressed. There is one pond at northeast side from the trace of shrine.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Only small amount of the brick and laterite fragments are confirmed. Looted hole is opened at the center of the mound.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Only small amount of the brick and laterite fragments are confirmed. Looted hole is open at the center of the mound.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Upper structure is completely destroyed. There are two looted holes on the mound. Brick structure is not confirmed. One pond is located nearby this mound at the east side.

Name: Phnom Banda
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: The structure is completely collapsed with a relatively big mound. This position appears be important because of the location at the center of western side of moat of Isanapura, and earthen dike (ancient road) starts from this mound in a southeast direction.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted holes
Description: There are many looted holes on the mound. The central hole is 1.7m deep and interior brick wall is partially confirmed. The moat opened by an earthen road to the east direction is 10m width, and southwest direction is 5m width.

Name: Toek Sampoch
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Upper structure is completely destroyed. Looted hole is in size of 2.7m (N-S) x 2.8m (E-W) and 1.6m deep. One part of foundation structure is confirmed in this hole. Under the brick structure is stacked laterite chips with soil. There is one pond at the east side of the structure. Sandstone fragment of the projection of a pedestal was found in the rice field at northeast direction of this mound (12/52/43, 105/0/59). This object was transported to the site storehouse.

Name: Toek Sampoch (same name as M.109)
Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Upper structure is completely destroyed. Presently a very low mound with small amount of brick and laterite fragments remains. There are many traces of looting holes. Deformed brick structure is partially confirmed in the central hole. This location is on the dike of the west moat.

Name: No name
Condition: Some brick are scattered
Description: Many brick were found when local people dug the ground. However, structure is unconfirmed. This location is nearby an artificial pond that is called Srah Sran and is the biggest pond in this group of monuments.

Name: Prasat Prohot
Condition: Mound but partial brick structure remains
Description: Upper structure is collapsed but some part of the interior brick wall remains. There are many sandstone elements scattered on and around the mound. Receiving stone of “Somasutra” remains. Cylindrical shape of sandstone elements requires evaluation. One pond is located at the east side.

Name: Toul Phum Boeng
Condition: Brick pieces are scattered
Description: There is no trace of structure but scattered bricks are concentrated in the immediate area.

Name: Toul Prasat
Condition: Remains with lower half of the interior wall
Description: This is the most southerly located building in this inventory. Interior brick wall still remains approximately 1.5m in height. Some parts of the pedestal are scattered. Small wooden shrine for the Nak Ta is constructed nearby the structure, and some of the stone elements are offered at the shrine.

Name: Toul Lvea
Condition: Mound with brick scattering.
Description: The mound is located in the overflow area of the Stung Sen. Brick paving structure is partially confirmed in the looted hole at the western side of the mound. Small amounts of brick and pebbles are scattered around. The mound is surrounded by a depressed area. An earthen dike might be connected to the mound from the western upland where the main temple area is located.

Name: No name
Composition: Single shrine or one component of Prasat Tao (M.026)
Condition: Mound with looted hole
Description: The mound is located in front of Prasat Tao (M.026). This is the similar relation with the M.023 and Prasat Yeai Poeun (M. 024). Looted hole is opened at the center of the mound, brick and pebble layer of structure is partially confirmed in this hole.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with looted hole
Description: Upper structure is completely destroyed. Many bricks are scattered around the mound. One fragment of the pedestal was found.

Name: No name
Condition: Small mound with brick
Description: Small amount of the bricks are scattered around low mound. There is no trace of the structure itself.

Name: No name
Condition: Small mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: The mound is covered by brick and some fragment of laterite. Central looted hole is opened to 2.1m depth, and brick structure is confirmed.

Name: No name
Condition: Small mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Upper structure is completely destroyed. Brick, laterite and pebbles are scattered around the mound. Looted hole is 1.3m in depth, and brick wall is confirmed at west and north side. The existence of a moat has not been confirmed, but surrounding area of the mound is slightly depressed.

Name: No name
Condition: Small mound with brick and laterite
Description: Small amount of brick and laterite fragment are scattered on the low mound. Many traces of the looting works are confirmed on the central area of the mound. The existence of the moat is unconfirmed but surrounding area of the mound is slightly depressed.

Name: Yeai Toav
Condition: Scattered blocks
Description: Building is completely destroyed and scattered elements such as pebbles, brick fragments and small amount of laterite is evidence of the existence of brick monuments. As there is no elevated mound and scattered elements distributed in the large area, it is difficult to identify the original location and size of this building.

Name: Prei Kamnorb
Condition: Scattered blocks
Description: The mound is located around 650m from M.122 to the NW direction and both of them are in Dang Antak village. There is one wooden Neak Ta shrine at the southwest side of this remnant. Building is completely destroyed and some scattered brick and laterite fragments are confirmed. The existence of some building by these scattered elements can be proposed.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with brick
Description: The mound is located to the north of M.042. Brick masonry is partially confirmed in the hole. Dimension of the hole is 1.8m (N-S) x 1.9m (E-W) and 1.6m deep.

Name: O Tlok Kakos (1)
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite
Description: The mound is located at the east side of stream O Tlok Kakos. The stream flows in a southerly direction and connects to the northeast corner of moat of “Isanapura”. The stream was completely dried up in April. Building is completely destroyed and no brick or laterite masonry was confirmed. Mound is slightly elevated. There are small amounts of brick fragments and laterite fragments are scattered. Many laterite chips covered the mound. Some sandstone fragments were found, but nearly all fragments are decayed. Only one stone is curiously curved in the shape of the lower half of a human face.

Name: O Tlok Kakos (2)
Condition: Mound with brick and laterite
Description: The mound is located northward from O Tlok Kakos (1) around 150m away and along stream O Tlok Kakos, on the west bank of this stream (). The mound was covered by heavy bush therefore confirmation of the condition could not be made. There are many medium size pebbles and brick fragments. One big hole is opened at the center of the mound.

Name: So Kgang
Condition: Long mound with brick and pebble
Description: This is a long mound with the long axis in the E-W direction. Many laterite blocks are scattered or positioned on the slope of the mound and a few blocks are scattered on the mound. By this situation, it is estimated that these laterite blocks are the step-like wall of this long basement structure. Almost every laterite blocks was broken and in fragments. However, some of them maintain the original shape. Especially step-like laterite layers can be confirmed at the southeast corner. Confirmation of small amounts of the brick fragments was made; it is difficult to suppose the existence of some brick building on this laterite basement structure. The scale of the mound is similar to the Proeng Thom (M090) at the center of city “Isanapura”. There is one small Neak Ta shrine on the mound.

Name: Toul Prasat
Composition: Three shrines are aligned in NNE-SSW direction.
Description: These three brick shrines are located on a large mound approximately 4m in height. The interval of these three shrines is 12m each. There are many rectangular ponds at the northern side of this site. Trees around this site were cut down in preparation to make rice fields by local people in 2005. Measures for protect this site from this destruction are required.

Condition: Mound with brick and looted hole
Description: Upper structure is completely collapsed. Only some bricks are scattered. Looted hole is opened at the center of the mound; size of this hole is 2.0m (N-S) x 3.0m (E-W) and 0.8m deep.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: Upper structure is completely collapsed. North and west side of vertical or under floor walls remain partially. West interior wall partially remains. Hole at the center of this wall is 3.2m (N-S) x 7m (E-W) and 1.4m deep. Scattered sandstone looks like broken door flame. This stone element was used as a grave marker at the later period or recently.

Condition: Mound with brick, laterite and looted hole
Description: The structure is most damaged of the three structures. Central looted hole is 1.2m in depth.

Name: Prasat Yeai Leak
Composition: Three shrines are aligned in north-south direction. Interval of each shrine is around 9.5m. Line of three shrines is slightly rotated in counterclockwise and probably this angle is similar to the angle of long bank at east side. These 3 shrines stand on one highland, the area is 38m (N-S) x 18m (E-W).
Description: This site is located about 200m east from the edge of the highland. There is a large pond, Srah Tapic, and long earthen bank (local people called simply "ancient road") at the eastern side from this site. Combination between temple and bank is similar as Prasat Sambor and Prasat Yeai Poeun, but these two elements are not on the same axis in this site. One of the scattering door jambs in the central shrine is 187cm in height. Therefore, it is supposed not as big a scale of building. Many sandstone elements are scattering around the mound. Some elements are supposed to be the style of pre-Angkor period, and some of them are Angkor period. For example, both style of the pedestal and colonnette were found, and base with the feet of lion is the style of Angkor period. Because of these mixed style of elements, we could theorize that this site was constructed in pre-Angkor period initially but renovated in the Angkor period.

Condition: Mound with brick
Description: Upper structure is completely collapsed. Small looted hole is opened at the center of the mound.

Condition: Mound with brick
Description: Some parts of the brick masonry remain, but it is too difficult to identify the form of plan of the original structure.

Condition: Mound with brick
Description: Small hole with brick masonry remains (0.5m deep). Large size of the bricks around 35cm long are mixed in the collapsed brick masonry.

Name: Wat Chas
Condition: Brick and laterite block are scattered.
Description: This site is located on the highland and near Chormas village, about 1 km from the dike that was supposed a part of ancient road to the north direction. There is no trace of shrines but some brick fragments and one laterite block are scattered around. Because the surrounding area is not flat and many small varied elevations, some structure will be expected to be found by clearance work. Small Neak Ta shrine stands on north side of this site.

Name: Toul Nak Ta Kieng
Condition: Mound with laterite
Description: Upper structure is completely collapsed. Only small amount of the laterite block is scattered around. Neak Ta shrine that are called “Nak Ta Kieng” is located 200m north from the mound.

Name: Nak Ta Chinik
Condition: Some laterite and sandstone blocks are placed.
Description: Local people believe that this site is the home (?) of Neak Ta. A few large laterite blocks are scattered. One square shape of pedestal and one laterite shape of pedestal are piled up on the laterite pavement. Furthermore another square shape of broken pedestal is scattered nearby. Some villagers say one pedestal with inscription and very large round shape of pedestal existed previously in this site.

Name: No name
Condition: Mound with brick
Description: Upper structure is completely collapsed. There are two holes (both of them are size at approximately 1.5m x 1.5m) on mounds. These two holes connected underground and there appears some brick masonry. This site is located at the north side of dike (ancient road) that is started from M129.
